summarization.a: HOMO orbitals at the transition state for the iodine release reaction, showing Ti1−Ti2 bonding interaction via sd orbital overlap, partial cleavage of Ti−I bonds, and a change in Ti coordination geometry from tetrahedral to trigonal bipyramidal.
summarization.b: HOMO orbitals at the product-like adsorption equilibrium, showing formation of the I₂ molecule, lengthened Ti−I bonds, and stabilization through enhanced overlap between I p orbitals and Ti sd orbitals.

data_extraction.a:
|Orbital Feature|Description|
|---------------|-----------|
|Transition State|Ti1−Ti2 bonding interaction observed due to sd orbital overlap; Ti1−I2 bond 3.14 Å; Ti2−I4 bond 2.80 Å; Ti coordination geometry changes from tetrahedral to trigonal bipyramidal.|
data_extraction.b:
|Orbital Feature|Description|
|---------------|-----------|
|Product-like Adsorption Equilibrium|I2 and I4 form I2 molecule; all Ti−I bonds longer than in reactant due to Ti rehybridization; structure stabilized by increased overlap between I p orbitals and Ti sd orbitals.|

a.question: Does the Ti−Ti bonding interaction appear during the transition state of the iodine release reaction?
a.answer: Yes. The HOMO orbital analysis shows that the Ti1−Ti2 bonding interaction is formed due to the overlap of their sd orbitals in the transition state.

b.question: How do the Ti−I bond lengths change from the transition state to the product-like adsorption equilibrium state?
b.answer: The Ti−I bonds become longer in the product-like state compared to the transition state, reflecting rehybridization of Ti and stabilization of the iodine molecule.

d.question: How does the HOMO orbital distribution in Figure 15 help in understanding the energetics and feasibility of the iodine release reaction in ALD of TiO2?
d.answer: The HOMO orbital distribution shows where electron density is concentrated during the transition state and product formation. In the transition state, the Ti−Ti bonding interaction and partial Ti−I bond cleavage indicate electron redistribution necessary to form I2. In the product, the extended Ti−I bond lengths and stabilized overlap with I p orbitals explain why the reaction is endothermic (57.8 kcal/mol) and has a high activation barrier (61.9 kcal/mol). This orbital insight helps rationalize the slow kinetics and strong energy requirements observed experimentally, supporting the mechanistic understanding of iodine release during ALD.

c.question: The Ti−I bonds become longer in the product-like state compared to the transition state, reflecting rehybridization of Ti and stabilization of the iodine molecule.
c.answer: Ti coordination geometry changes from tetrahedral to trigonal bipyramidal in the transition state, Ti−Ti bonding forms in the transition state, Partial cleavage of Ti−I bonds in the transition state, I₂ molecule formation in the product state, Ti−I bonds lengthen in the product state, Enhanced overlap between I p orbitals and Ti sd orbitals in the product
